The balloons look cute-the cow print is nice. However as soon as you open the plastic packaging you are hit with a SUPER STRONG WHIFF of PAINT THINNER. There is NO WAY these balloons were made with any safe/legal paint/coloring/material. If I had a baby in the house, I would have immediately thrown them outside. As it was, we only blew up a few and the smell was so nauseating we felt sick and the entire 1st floor of our house smelled like we spilled paint thinner. I put the unused ones in a plastic bag and sealed it tight after deciding it was not worth the risk to my health to blow them up or have them in my house. The house STILL SMELLED like poison for several hours and into the next morning from the 4-5 balloons we did blow up. I would venture to say this is made with toxic material and it’s definitely not worth exposing yourself to for cute balloons. The factory where these are made should be looked into; I worry about the workers!

Every game I want to play needs the select button to play, so they can't be played. That makes it useless at the end of the day.  Very sad to have been so close. The aspect ratio is also WAY off on game boy games. As for the other games, they stuttered a lot and were very hard to play because of that. Screen is great, battery wasn't, but I played it plugged in anyway. I contacted the seller and they were quick to respond, but said the buttons could not be remapped. Bad programming has made something that would have otherwise been a ten out of five stars for me, only one.

When I saw that there were light kits for some of the Lego kits,  I thought that was a neat idea. Most of the kits, however, were not for Lego sets that my family had. Then I saw the Boba Fett one that my 23 year old son had recently assembled, so I had to order it. It comes with an instruction booklet that shows you step by step with pictures. I assisted my husband with the task. You do have to take parts of the Lego masterpiece apart to do the job. The wires are very thin and once the job is finished, they're not very noticeable. They could have been completely hidden if we had run the first wire along the opposite side of the brick. Lesson learned. By the time we were finished, we were glad to be done and didn't want to go back and change it. The whole thing probably took less than 30 minutes. We were a little slow at times, trying to decipher the instructions to make sure that we were removing the correct piece and such. The actual lights have adhesive strips on them to keep them in place. You do have to plug the wire ends into a piece so everything is linked together. It requires 3 AAA batteries, not included. The end results are great! Our son was very pleased with how it looks.

As a child, I loved pop-up books, so of course I love all of the LEGO pop-up book building kits. The first one (see [[ASIN:B07GYB8D34 LEGO Pop-Up Book]] originated as a fan model submitted to the LEGO Ideas fan-voting site (if you want to participate, just google for LEGO Ideas).  This DISNEY MULAN’S STORYBOOK ADVENTURES 43174 BUILDING KIT (New 2020 (124 Pieces)) is a smaller, simpler version of the original pop-up book, but it’s still a lot of fun.  The basic pop-up feature is the arched bridge that goes from the center--the book’s stationary spine, where Mulan’s buildings are--to the little “tea service” island in the left half of the book.  The bridge goes up and down on a LEGO hinge, and snaps into place when the book is completely open.  There are LEGO studs strategically placed around the open book, so that the three dolls, Mulan’s pony, and Mulan’s cricket cage can all be firmly attached at once.  This way, they don’t rattle around loose (or get lost) when the book is closed to be put away.  When the book is open for play, the dolls fit on the bridge, under the arches, and can stand anywhere on the book surface.  The assembly instructions are reasonably easy to follow--just look closely at the pictures.  I had to take my book covers apart and reassemble them because I got the left (blue water) and right (white land) sides switched, so that the water was on the wrong side when the book was opened.  There are quite a few stickers, but only a few that need to be carefully placed on LEGO elements.  The instructions aren’t terribly clear on where to place the stickers that go onto the book’s interior surfaces (e.g., the swimming Koi fish, the lily pads, Mulan’s bed roll, the campfire, the cooking supplies).  You can consult the box photos, or even the photos in the online LEGO Shop, if you want extra guidance for these.  One of my favorite features is the two little tassels or bells that swing from Mulan’s temple-like building.  I don’t know whether this is an entirely new LEGO element, or whether it has appeared in other sets, but it was new to me.  There are enough LEGO pieces in the set to make the book interesting to assemble; and the book is fun to play with after it’s built.

As with many others this spring, I've been doing lots of puzzles. This is a vibrant high quality puzzle. It's the first one that I've had lately that has had any kind of smell to it. This THAMES & KOSMOS GIANT DINOSAUR SKELETON KIT is a child’s building toy straight out of a parent’s worst nightmare!  IF you are able to assemble the model, the large size is the only good thing about it.  There aren’t many pieces; the pieces all lack detail; the front leg pieces are ridiculous, pathetic cutouts; and the skull and jaw are tricky to assemble.  Perhaps my kit was defective, but the pieces were so badly manufactured, it was NOT possible to even build the T-rex model.  Only 3 of the 9 rib pieces would slide into the slots intended for them.  The other 6 rib pieces were simply too big to fit into their slots.  The only way to make them fit would be (1) to file down the rib pieces to narrow them, or (2) to file the slots to widen them.  Prying on the slots to open them wider only met with failure.  Other joints in the model (e.g., the spine, the hips, the large legs) were hard to fit together, and took tremendous hand strength to press together and join.  A young child would NOT be able to “snap together” these pieces at all.  My dinosaur stood up--it balances on its tail when you position the legs just so--but a dinosaur without ribs is not anything you want to display.  Even the manual was disappointing. The building instructions are all right, but the accompanying “scientific” explanations are nothing more than a collection of simplistic illustrated factoids.  As a child’s gift, this building kit is bound to disappoint, and is all too likely to produce tears of frustration and unhappiness on a child's special day or holiday.

My nephew is a Nerf connoisseur, with an arsenal that is the envy of most of the neighborhood kids, so I asked him to help me evaluate this new Nerf gun.  He says that at the time he got it, it was new on the market, and he was excited to try it out.  He found that it requires four D-batteries, which are not included, but fortunately his parents had some on hand.  The gun was pretty big, but not unwieldy.  The setup, he says, was pretty easy.  After inserting the batteries, you simply insert the clip into the slot.  Then insert your darts, and you are ready to fire.  To fire, you pull the rev trigger, and then the trigger itself.  Compared to other blasters, my nephew found that despite its size, it is very customizable.  It fires very fast and the loading feature is a big plus; it is unique to this particular blaster.  In short, he would "definitely recommend it" to anyone who likes Nerf.

The small size of this LEGO ARCHITECTURE GREAT WALL OF CHINA BUILDING KIT (551 Piece) is deceptive--it’s more challenging to build than you might think, and it includes some very interesting building techniques.  I’m totally awed by the clever way that the wall is chained together and secured, so that it meanders up and along the grassy ridge.  The kit took me about 2¼ hours to build, working steadily but taking time to read the factoids in the building instructions as I went along.  How interesting to learn that “sticky rice” used in the mortar contributed to the wall’s longevity!  (Because this kit is part of the LEGO Architecture series, the instruction booklet has extras such as photos of the actual monument, a few pages of history, and some structural schematics.)  The kit includes a large quantity of beautiful dark green bricks (hunter green) that make me want to think of something else to make with them--or with that color, if I don’t ever want to disassemble the model.  I’m keeping the model for now, because it is very decorative when complete. (A nice feature is that there are two different “Great Wall of China” ID plates--one in English, one in Chinese.)
My grandson loves playing with it.  The tune on it is the same as the tune of the Fisher Price school bus.  This is well worth the money.

When My Two year old Granddaughter saw this Radio Flyer Red Trike, her eyes lite up,BIG smile on her face,and she was speechless, for a Second!.. It is good looking. Very cute. She loves it.  Although the Plastic tires are a disappointment with out groves in them. Since we live in the mountain, (as they do) on a few acres, the only cement to ride this on is our walkways around the house, I Then thought she could ride this Radio flyer trike on our long loop Dirt drive-way, but its a bit hilly and the plastic tires are not good for traction on the Dirt, or any light mud going up a hill. The wheels spin. With out traction its not as fun for her as I thought it would be. We have to push her around in some places. The good part about the plastic tires are,they will not be popped by the stickers that pop the other bikes the older kids ride around here.  It was easier putting the Trike together then it was getting the box open, and that is no exaggeration ! It wasn't taped together, it was Glued, and two boxes the Trike came in, It certainly is not damaged. This comes with very good detailed instruction how to put this together..If my Husband can put this together anyone can.  The little storage compartment in the back is durable plastic, and adorable for girls or boys. My Granddaughter put her dolly in there, with some other goodies. The seat will adjusts.  If you live in a area with sidewalks, or have a Large deck the kids will love ridding this Trike, or even on level grass.  I just want to mention, make sure your children have shoes on. I bought a larger Radio flyer for my older grandson (4yr) and he kept hitting his toes on the cement when peddling . They gave that bike away in less then a month. (his toes where bleeding). They lived in Hawaii, and he was usually bare-foot.

I'm a little bit disappointed in this product, I used to love items from the Sharper Image and associate it with quality branding. To be fair- I LOVE the concept, the cars are GREAT and a lot of fun to smash and reassemble. The downside is actually the remotes! The knob used to control the car steering broke on one of them the day we got it, the other knob broke a couple of days later. Didn't even last an entire week. We're talking, SNAP sound and bits of plastic feel out, and now it is really difficult to steer. Meanwhile even when it worked fine, I was surprised at how many batteries this needs. 6 AAAs and 4 AA, not rechargeable battery packs like other RC cars I've owned. Such a good concept, my kids LOVED it while it was working, but clearly this is a product with room for improvement.

I ordered this toy for my cousin's toddler, who is just a bit over 16 months old.  Joey adored the toy from the very beginning.  He quickly caught on, with the help of older brother Brandon (who is nearly five), how to keep the puppy reading and singing by turning the pages of the book.  In fact, it was Brandon who figured out the toy and taught Joey how to use it completely, a testament to the user-friendliness and age-appropriateness of this toy.  I thought the price might be a little high, but I must say it had provided hours of fun and laughter, as well as a good learning experience for Joey.  I would guess that this would make a good "from the grandparents" toy, simply because of the price.  I definitely recommend it--and the batteries are included, something I wish would become industry standard.  Update:  After speaking with people who are more accustomed to purchasing toys than I am, I am going to change my opinion of the price.  One person I spoke with said, "Thirty five bucks?  For a puppy that sings and reads and moves?  That is really reasonable."  So I think I may be living in the previous century when it comes to price.  This is a delightful toy, and I think any toddler would love it.

Hide & Seek was always one of my favorite games when I was a child. I liked the new twist to the game. The directions are pretty easy to understand. You hide the device, then activate it. It will make a beeping type sound (that's not too annoying). The frequency of the beeps and the sound strength can be changed depending on the level you select. It's obviously much easier to find it when it's loudly beeping every few seconds! It becomes more challenging when the volume is softer and the beeps are spaced out more. I thought this would be a fun game for my young adult children to play with their younger cousins. They had a blast! There are some other games you can play, too, such as Hot Potato & Simon. Batteries are not included.

If you're looking for a wonderful thinking game, this is an excellent purchase to get started. It comes with a Go instruction booklet as well, which hits all the high points of gameplay without being too long or boring. If you read your way through the entire book (about 20 illustrated pages) you will be well on your way to understanding most aspects of the game.  Several tips to note about this board:  It has 9x9 grid on one side and 13x13 on the other, which makes it the perfect board for beginners or those learning Go. These smaller sizes are normally used by less experienced players to learn the basics of the game, without being overwhelmed by the length of a full 19x19 match. A 9x9 game, depending on how long the players take to think about their moves, will take about 10 to 20 minutes, and a 13x13 game takes about 20 to 40 minutes.  The wood is spruce and is very good quality for a beginner's board. I have not used it for very long so I don't know how long it would take the lines on the board to wear off or show signs of use. However, it's still in pristine quality from when I bought it, and I've used it quite thoroughly. Any dust or dirt can be easily wiped off with a dry or slightly damp cloth (it comes with instructions on how to care for the board).  Of primary consideration is the quality of the Go stones that come with this board. If you don't have any stones, this is also a great purchase because the stones are rust-proof and will never crack, break, or corrode. They are nice and big, so they are easy to hold and become used to playing Go with. Another plus is that it comes with enough stones for a 19x19 game, so if you later choose to purchase a full sized board, you'll have enough stones to play with.  Overall, a great purchase if you want to jump into learning how to play Go.

[[VIDEOID:df61075bc0172b4d34aec64cd5ddd45b]] The concept of smart cubes are really cool: They connect over Bluetooth low power protocol to an app so that your movements are digitized. This opens up possibilities like training exercises and real-time online competitions. The app is actually pretty excellent here, unlike another cube we tried, this one actually has competitors and it seems to work (however my son is suspicious that they might not be real players. They could be AI, because how many people could there really be using this app at the same time he is?). However, the hardware seems buggy on this. Sometimes the cube will move and the app won't register that movement. Try it again later with the same move, and it does. In the video here, my son demonstrates that it completely loses calibration as he solves the cube. We tried the app on a few different devices in our home, with processors from Qualcomm Snapdragon and other's with Samsung's Exynos (flagship Galaxy S level hardware). It doesn't seem to be the phone/tablet that is the problem, but rather that the cube isn't always registering the movement to send to the app. This causes things to get out of alignment relatively quickly, especially when speed solving, and renders the entire experience frustrating. This had so much potential, we're really disappointed.

The great strength--and weakness--of this CREATIVITY FOR KIDS SPARK!LAB SMITHSONIAN "INVENT A MOTORIZED ROBOT" MODEL KIT is that there are NO step-by-step instructions for building even one simple model robot.  The idea of the kit is that a kid will figure out how to use the included parts to assemble a heavy paper robot of her/his own design.  The included booklet only gives general hints on how to use the parts.  The illustrations on the box give further hints for assembling the parts into different-looking robots.  The parts include:  foam gears; plastic soda straws; bendable wire; gold metallic tape; glue; paper fasteners; punch-out, foldable, printed paper robot body parts; a motor wired to a pair of LED lights and a battery box; decorative stickers; and other items.  The box shows a complete inventory of the parts, except that it doesn't make it clear that the boxy robot body parts are only made of lightweight paper, and that they arrive in flat sheets (although the punch-out body parts do have score lines to help with folding them into three-dimensional components).  For some reason, the batteries for powering the motor (two AA alkaline batteries) are NOT included.  For the right child, this may be a fun toy.  It is relatively inexpensive (about $20 on Amazon) for a building toy.  However, I think that many children would be as baffled as I was by this building set.  How do you attach the gear wheels?  Do you use the plastic straws for axles?  If so, how do you attach the axles to the robot body so that they will turn?  Where should I place the motor?  What, if anything, will the motor power besides the big gear wheels?  Many of the included parts are merely decorative, and struck me as being a waste of time.  I don't know whether the motor will power anything besides wheels (e.g., robot arms), but I do know that the motor's usefulness is severely limited by the very short length of wire between it and the battery box.  The two LED lights are attached to the battery box with short wires, too, so they always have to be placed close to the motor.  The lightweight paper components will not stand up to being taken apart and reassembled into different configurations--at least not very often.  Overall, I don't think this kit will teach anything about robotics.  It will teach that gears attached to a motor will turn when the motor is turned on; and it will teach that you can rearrange decorative items to make robots that look different.  Because of its limited educational value and lack of instructions that make the assembly task fun all by itself, I rate this building toy at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).

My family enjoys playing games. We discovered cooperative ones a few years ago and those have become our favorite. Each round takes up to 5 minutes and there are 5 rounds to the game. My 19 year old son was the one who read the directions and told us what to do. Most of our rounds took about 4 minutes, though one round ended up with just fourteen seconds left when we were done. The game is really quick and slightly chaotic. It got a bit messy in between rounds because we were just throwing the cards down on the table when we played them, but it was pretty fast to sort them back in between rounds because they are color coded. After the first round, my 16 year old daughter was the first one to say how fun she thought it was. That was unexpected because she was the least excited about playing it in the first place. The family has already decided that this is a game we'll take with us on our next beach/cabin trip.

This REX’S REXPLORER! 70835 BUILDING KIT (from LEGO The Movie 2; 1172 Pieces) is a big kit that takes a while to build (a good thing, in my opinion).  The worst thing about it is the color choice of dark blue for the bricks.  Dark blue always makes the printed building instructions hard to follow--the printed black brick outlines merge right in with the blue of the bricks unless you have strong lighting.  The best thing about the kit is the little raptor figures, which have vicious-looking moveable jaws.  At times the build is repetitive, because the Rexplorer has four wheels and corners to complete (like any LEGO vehicle).  I ran into one problem with the build:  For the four corners beginning at Step 115, you must add the assembled piece with studs DOWN to make a solid build.  I originally added the piece with the studs sideways, which didn’t look quite right, and which made the area unstable later (pieces were easily dislodged with play).  The correct placement isn’t clear from looking at the Step 115 page (see photos).  When built correctly, the Rexplorer is a robust model that holds together well.  Once built, this set has a lot of play potential.  The Rexplorer has a cargo hold that opens at the side, and it’s loaded with stud- and bolt-shooting weapons.  The vehicle also has cockplts that open to hold raptors or minifigs.  The set has Rex and Emmett minifigures with weapons, two brick-built Plantimals, and 2 raptor figures, plus a big weapon for a raptor to carry on its back.

I’m one of the kids that had the ‘real’ or original Creepy Crawley machines from the 1960’s, and my daughter had one in the 1990’s.  This one is not even close to those!!  It’s awful! First you have to place 3 plastic “mites” (smaller bugs) into a rubbery cauldron, and microwave 10 seconds.  Then place the cauldron on top of the machine and squeeze the hot liquid into the mold.  So, you basically turn little bugs into bigger ones.  (EYE ROLL…). Then you let it cool until the mold can be removed.  It came with two molds so you can make two whole bugs an hour (EYE ROLL, again).  The mold latch began to fail after the first bug, so it’s going to become even more useless (if that’s possible) with the making of a couple more bugs.  This Creepy Crawley machine may be impressive to someone who have never seen or had the real thing… but it’s not to me.  I’m returning it for a full refund, and I’m on the search of a good used Original Creepy Crawley machine with hot temp metal plates and GOOP!!

This  PEGBOARD SET WITH PATTERN CARD comes with lots of big, colorful, stackable pegs, but the foam base is something of a disaster.  Out of the box, the holes are too small for the pegs, so the instructions say to "condition" the base by inserting the pegs all the way through the holes and leaving them there overnight.  This does enlarge the holes in the foam base, but the holes still want to close up.  This makes the pegs difficult to insert into the base for play.  I purchased this set for my husband to use for occupational therapy at home, in conjunction with a Saebo glove,  to help restore functionality to his stroke-damaged hand.  Unfortunately, the set is useless for this purpose, because of the effort it takes to insert the pegs (even if you have full use of both hands).  I think it would be equally difficult for a child to use this set, at least until the foam base becomes well-worn so that the peg holes will stop closing up.  A couple of pegboard sets that do work for my husband's hand therapy are the [[ASIN:B0009K30YY Learning Resources Stacking Shapes Peg Board]], and the [[ASIN:B008E18S60 Hape Color and Shape Sorter]].  Because of its disappointing foam base, I rate the Pegboard Set With Pattern Card at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).

I love building toys, perpetual motion machines, and Rube Goldberg inventions, so of course I like this MARBLE MANIA ZOOMERANG BUILDING KIT.  It's not as much fun as it ought to be, though.  First, it's quite fiddly to put together.  The black "universal" connectors must be oriented correctly (with holes outward) whenever they support ramp clips.  The purple tower pieces come in several sizes, and choosing a wrong size piece will create a tower that's the wrong height so that the ramps will slant incorrectly.  The clear tube surrounding the orange spiral lifter has a light gray connector at the bottom that must be placed "just so" or the marbles won't be able to enter the tube.  Second, playing with the setup is constantly interrupted because cranking the spiral lift causes the gears to disconnect frequently.  I found that I could achieve fairly constant motion if I held the support piece for the lower gear (the green one) down with my hand against the black (gridded) base, but even then the gears would unexpectedly disengage--just when I was beginning to enjoy the perpetual motion rhythm.  The "Rube Goldberg" aspect of the toy is a lot of fun.  The marbles climb to the top of the spiral, move quickly down the ramps, and circle around or drop straight through the blue funnel to begin their journey anew.  The spiral lifter will hold as many as eight marbles--the building kit comes with three marbles--and you can get more marbles going through if you can solve the problem of the disengaging gears.  The marbles occasionally jump the track, but not often.  The assembly instructions aren't as clear as LEGO instructions, but they're not very hard to follow.  The black universal connections have a little red dot in front of them when they need to be oriented with the holes forward (see my customer photo).  The parts are reasonably well made.  One of my connector pieces was flawed, but it worked all right as a support piece (see my customer photo).  The $19.99 price seemed about right for this toy.  This kit did in fact require a couple of hours for assembly.  Usually I can assemble a structure with the equivalent number of pieces much faster.  The black universal connector pieces in particular were uncomfortable to handle.  I believe that this kit can be combined with other TechnoGears Marble Mania kits to create a more complex marble maze.  However, I wasn't interested in building onto this particular kit, because of the problem with the spiral crank and the disengaging gears.  I rate MARBLE MANIA ZOOMERANG at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

This drone is the worst we've ever tested. We've tested cheap ones and expensive ones and this one was the biggest disappointment and it absolutely is a safety concern! It randomly just stops responding to the remote and even with a full charge! Unlike what it claims- it doesn't just land when it loses signal- sometimes it'll continue to climb up into the air way over the house, sometimes it'll fly into things including almost hitting people at the park!  We got the first one and charged it fully and took it out back. Within a minute it went crazy and stopped responding and flew into the house, shattering 2 of the blades. We replaced those and tried again only to have it go unresponsive and run into our patch of sunflower stalks where it continued to run into my sunflowers cutting at them and after a few seconds of that it managed to burn up one of it's motors!  So we got a replacement and charged it and took it to the park. Within a couple minutes it went haywire and started flying at people when we weren't touching anything and it wouldn't let us land it or anything. Literally unresponsive!  We calibrated it multiple times and nothing helps. This thing is just a fancy looking dangerous paperweight. We've tested drones 1/3rd of the price that were way better made!

No stranger to Legos, I've been assembling various themed kits since the early 1980s. I was also a child when the original Stars Wars movies came out, and over the years have owned various Stars Wars merchandise like lunchboxes, toys, action figures, Viewmaster Slides, and other collectibles.  So when I was offered the chance to test drive the LEGO Millennium Falcon, I was overjoyed.  It had been years since I last assembled a Lego set.  The level of detail on this is amazing - it has an opening cockpit, interior access, hidden compartments, spring-loaded external weapons and five figures including C3PO and R2D2.  With nearly 1,400 pieces, this is not a kit for the short of patience or faint of heart, but it DOES make a fantastic project the whole family can enjoy together.  (and if you lose / misplace the directions, you can handily download / print from Lego's official website if you search by kit number 7527).  The perfect gift for any Star Wars fan, whether you're a child or a child at heart!

This CIRCUIT CUBES BRIGHT LIGHTS STEM LEARNING TOY KIT (Multicolor) has a lot of good features.  It has three circuit cubes (battery, motor, LED) that can be arranged in different ways.  The LED cube is the star of the kit, but it’s worth having for lighting up LEGO builds that go way beyond the projects explained in the kit booklet.  All of the cubes connect with LEGO studs.  Unlike many toy kits advertised as educational STEM kits, this kit has the building instructions packed inside the box.  There’s no need to go online and join a club (and get your personal info entered into a database) to get the instructions, although there is a Circuit Cube Club available if a child is interested.  The booklet with the projects is very clear and easy to follow.  The projects include flashlight; hand-crank flashlight; telescope flashlight; gobo; lighthouse; night light; spinning light car; desk lamp; shadow puppets; and motorized gobo.  The kit has lots of different things for a child to try.  It isn’t a kit that takes 5 minutes to assemble, 5 minutes to see what the build does, and 5 minutes to tire of it and set it aside never to be played with again.  The kit includes a small drawstring bag that is great for keeping the cubes and other parts together once the kit is unpacked.  The one thing that I didn’t like about the kit is that the booklet doesn’t explain anything about how circuits work.  Whatever the child learns is going to come from trial-and-error experimentation with arranging the cubes.  I still don’t know why my original connections for the hand-crank flashlight didn’t work, except for the observation that they crossed to opposite posts instead of going in a straight daisy chain sequence.  Something to watch out for if you’re buying more than one of this series of kits is that you’ll get the same three basic circuit cubes in some or all of them.

Rarely do I write reviews, especially positive ones, but this one deserves it. I purchased this product for my 3 year old niece so that she would have something to do at our big family Thanksgiving. She was excited as she looked forward to opening it (after 3 more bites of sweet potatoes). We unwrapped the box together as she sat on my lap. The first thing that I noticed was the multitude of colors -an almost overwhelming array- which immediately sparked creativity and imagination in both of us. She doesn't like to get messy. This is not at all the "messy" type of clay. It sticks to itself and nothing else (floor, tablecloth, hands and face). It even has a pleasant, almost cotton candy, aroma. I was so focused on the mindful, stress relieving activity of squishing and snapping the clay, I almost didn't notice the hilarity of what was happening all around the rest of the dining room table. Every member of my family, from age 3 to 66 was playing with the clay. There were enough individual packs to go around and we still have a few unopened colors to delve into next time we gather. The turkey was cooked perfectly, the butternut squash soup was delicious, and Lord knows I love that pumpkin pie; but, the real hit of the party was Sago Brothers Magic Clay.

purchased for my grandsons to use when visiting..... it is large.... large enough for an an adult to get into.  The material is a cotton canvas... not terribly thick, but not too thin in my opinion.  It was relatively easy to put together... I like that this is a bottom floor attached.  It is quite popular to stay grandma's and camp out.... I added some "nap pads" which are perfect for the 5 and 2 year old.  I also added a stuffed bear and alligator and a camp lantern that is a lantern or a star map night light.  Honestly, I keep it up in the corner of my living room so it is ready for any visit. It does come with a handy bag that has a handle if you'd like to store it when not in use.  The poles are wood... a pine I believe... very sturdy.  I would recommend this teepee...  I always update if there are any issues that may be helpful. 3/25/2016 UPDATE:  ...so I just finished deconstructing the teepee and felt an undate with tips might be helpful... first... you will never get the canvas teepee back into the canvas bag provided... it's like folding/rolling a really thick fitted sheet and trying to get into a tube sock.... BUT the wooden poles fit nicely into the canvas bag along with the plastic connector pieces... it would have been nice if the connectors gripped the poles better and stayed connected during the deconstruct phase... however... I just removed all the holed top pieces and then turned the teepee upside down and shook it sort of like a pillow case... the lower pieces eventually come out...  I just rolled the teepee up, put all the poles and connector pieces in the bag and set in a large drawer I have.  I would have lowered my rating one star for the deconstruct hassle, but honestly, my grandsons love it so much I just can't. The quality is such that this is going to last for many years if we take care of it.  It's just a matter of learning how to use it in an efficient manner.  Also... I have hard wood floors, so I went to a local carpet store and purchased some carpet padding.  I cut into a circle and place it under the teepee when I have it set up... I worry less about bonked heads that way - grandsons 5 years and 2 years.  Ok!  Hope this is indeed helpful info!

Root Vue Farm is a child's toy designed to educate kids on one of the wonders of nature. It is planting device that allows children a chance to watch vegetables grow and see what they look like below the surface of the ground.  When I purchased Root Vue Farm, I intended it to be a good educational lesson and in some ways, it does offer some educational value. It succeeds at getting kids to think more about plants and from a different perspective than usual. Kids and adults are all accustomed to looking at plants above the ground, but not below the surface where the edible parts of many vegetables are found. In this regard, Root Vue Farm is a good addition to a child's toy chest and it seemed like a good purchase.  The problems with Root Vue Farm, however, become evident when you put it together and plant the seeds. Taking it from the box, the first thing I didn't like was the construction. It is very cheaply made, using styrofoam to form the box that holds the soil and seeds. There is an acrylic viewing window, which is fine, but I couldn't believe the manufacturer made the main part of the toy from styrofoam. The use of plastic would have been better, but it appears the manufacturer was trying to cut costs in any way possible.  Next, we added the seeds and soil, watered it down, and waited for the seeds to grow. The first problem we encountered was with mold. The excessive moisture leads to mold growth and since the farm is made from styrofoam, you cannot easily keep it clean. At least with a plastic box, the mold could be wiped off. But you cannot clean this very easily and it stinks.  Waiting for the seeds to sprout also proved frustrating. The seeds do sprout in a matter of days, but they did not reach the point where the vegetables could be viewed through the viewing window. I also noticed weed- like growth on the top of the soil. I don't know where it came from, but there it was, covering the top of the soil.  Educational toys are great and the concept of growing vegetables and observing their growth progress and root network still has some appeal. I may look for another toy with the same concept and better construction, but I will not bother with Root Vue Farm again. Nasty mold growth, styrofoam, and other annoyances make this an educational experience to forget.

What I like about this LEGO DISNEY PRINCESS MULAN’S Training Day 41151 BUILDING KIT (104 Piece)—which is obviously marketed to girls—is that it features a strong, active female.  Mulan is at home, but her home features a sword, a catapult, and a spinning training dummy, not just a cherry tree in bloom, a fan, and a tea set.  The foal Khan is included, though Mulan can’t ride (she’s wearing a skirt).  What I don’t like about this set is the “Build and Swap” balloon on the box.  LEGO pieces are generally interchangeable, so it made me think that this set was part of a Mulan series of similar sets.  But it isn’t—the other 2018 Disney Princess sets are Sleeping Beauty’s Fairytale Castle, Ariel’s Royal Celebration, Cinderella’s Dream Castle, and Elsa’s Market Adventure.  None of these are compatible, theme-wise, with the Mulan set.  Apparently “Build and Swap” merely refers to the fact that some of the completed elements (e.g., cherry tree, catapult) can swap positions in Mulan’s home.  (See my photo.)  This doesn’t seem interesting enough to warrant special advertising, especially advertising that suggests the child needs to collect more sets for swapping.  Priced about $15 on Amazon, this small LEGO set is affordable as a gift, but isn’t exactly cheap. However, anything with the “Disney” trademark always costs more, and it’s a cute little set that Mulan fans should enjoy assembling and playing with.

This works ok.  The disc is metal and very heavy.  But the plastic base for the mirror is far too flimsy and lightweight to accommodate the disc without a lot of flexing and popping and clapping noise.  All of this  means lost energy which reduces spin time.  The product just doesn't implement well the design the patent describes.  I wanted to better stabilize the base, so under running hot water I very carefully removed the mirror from the base.  Perhaps not something I would recommend.  As I was doing so, the smell of whatever adhesive was used literally made me light-headed.  I've used a lot of epoxies and adhesives, but none that smelled as strong as this, like really strong gasoline.  The adhesive was soft and spongy, not fully cured.  I carefully removed all of  whatever it was and filled the gap between the base and the bottom of the mirror with air-dry clay to fill the gaps and to increase the weight of the base overall.  It did help to increase spin time somewhat and got rid of the annoying galloping-horse-playing-steel-drum sounds.

I’m a big fan of the Thames & Kosmos building kits--especially the ones that teach STEM principles.  However, this THAMES & KOSMOS PEPPER MINT IN THE FANTASTIC UNDERWATER SCIENCE VOYAGE EXPERIMENT KIT is definitely NOT one of my favorites.  The experiments do teach about the physical properties of water (e.g., siphoning, hydraulics), but they are boring--mostly they require operating a syringe to push water through tubing.  The boat is easy to build.  The parts are stamped onto thin sheets of wood.  You punch the parts out of the sheets, and the edges come out smooth and clean.  The parts then lock together without requiring glue or other fasteners.  The assembly instructions are included in a booklet that contains a silly adventure/discovery story that features the girl character Pepper Mint.  The story is interrupted from time to time with instructions for science experiments.  Each experiment has a good, easy-to-understand explanation of “what’s happening” (e.g., water can move things because, unlike air, it cannot be compressed).  The kit is apparently aimed at 11-year-old girls (Pepper Mint’s age), but the story and the experiments seem rather simplistic for that age group.  I do wish that Thames & Kosmos had chosen a more serious name than “Pepper Mint” for their girl scientist.  This kit has some teaching value, but it trivializes science for girls.

I purchased the Men are from Mars game for me and my boyfriend.  However, I was misled by the packaging. The box says the game is for couples 18 and older.  I assumed one couple (2 people) could play. Unfortunately, my assumption was wrong. Other games say for children 3 and up and then say 1-3 players, etc.  This box doesn't give you a player count. Since we don't have another couple to play this game with, I just wasted my money.  The manufacturer needs to change their packaging to say for 2 - 4 couples over 18 years.  I'm extremely disappointed.  The game board folds in four and looks like it would easily wear out if you used it a lot. However, if I had the appropriate amount of participants, it looks like the game would be good for a few laughs and some healthy fun.  I suspect you couldn't play it over and over as eventually you'll have answered all the questions.  What you really need is a steady dose of different couples to play the game with so that the game stays interesting. However, if you don't have the requested participants, you've just wasted your money as the game requires 2-4 couples.

The novel thing about this HOT WHEELS TRACK BUILDER ROCKET LAUNCH CHALLENGE PLAYSET is the three-stage play experience.  You launch a car through the loop arrangement to hit the rocket and collapse the loop; then you launch a car to jump the gap and raise the liftoff ramp; and finally you launch a car to launch the rocket.  The Stunt set has clear instructions for assembly, and if you follow them carefully, the stunt works exactly as promised.  The stunt is fun by itself, but also connects to the track in your existing Hot Wheels layout, if you have one.  The stunt uses standard Hot Wheels cars.  It comes with only one car, but it’s best with three cars, so that you don’t have to retrieve the car after each stage of play.

This LEGO CITY RACE BOAT TRANSPORTER BUILDING SET ((60254) New 2020, 238 Pieces) is fairly easy to build--the difficulty is what I’d call “normal” for a City-themed set.  City sets are among my favorites, because they include fire trucks, police stations, buses, trains, beachgoer minifigs, space stations, and all the rest of the things that a bustling city (or the airspace above it) needs.  This particular set is aimed at ages 5 and up.  The set contains all the pieces required to build three separate items:  a semi-trailer truck (for towing the race boat), a trailer for hauling the race boat, and a race boat.  (You can use the truck’s trailer hitch to tow other things that you build, of course.)  The “Xtreme” graphics are NOT printed on the pieces, but are applied with stickers.  (I hate stickers, too.)  Applying stickers is made easier if you first stick a corner to something long and thin (e.g., a spudger or a plastic knife) so that you can better control the placement on the element.  A good thing about LEGO stickers is that they can be removed and replaced (like Post-Its) if you get them misaligned.  The LEGO elements are compatible with other City sets and with all regular LEGO sets (but not with DUPLO sets, which have extra-large pieces for very young builders).  The Race Boat Transporter set is another fine addition to the extensive collection of City sets.

This BEYBLADE BURST EVOLUTION SWITCHSTRIKE STARTER PACK (Genesis Valtryek V) contains a Genesis Valtryek V (a right-spin top), and a launcher that can be used for either left-spin or right-spin tops.  You will need to purchase a “battle stadium” separately (e.g., [[ASIN:B01F6VS3ZG Beyblade Burst Beystadium]]).  The parts for Burst Evolution tops can be interchanged to create custom tops with different strengths and weaknesses.  I would strongly advise any adult who is purchasing one of these tops for a youngster to find out exactly which model it is that the kid wants.  The tops perform very differently, depending on their weight and on features such as the type of tip (e.g., rollerball).  The back of the toy card gives details on the features (e.g., type (attack or defense), weight, endurance, etc.) of the included top.  Hasbro’s battling tops are fun toys, but they have a learning curve.  The included instructions are so brief as to be almost useless, though they do explain how to assemble the top.  (There are detailed instructions and videos available on the toy manufacturer’s web site.  Go to the official Hasbro site, choose “Brands”, and then “Beyblade Burst”.)  It took some practice to get a good “Rip” (launch).  It also took me awhile to figure out that the launcher wouldn’t work unless the strip was inserted in the correct slot (e.g., right for a right-spin top), that the top needed to be rotated slightly onto the launcher, and that the tops were meant to be dropped into the stadium (not started on the stadium floor).  These tops require two people and two tops for play, because the tops are intended to be launched simultaneously for each battle.  I think that single players can use the accompanying Beyblade Burst app (available for iOS and Android), which adds the tops you own to your collection in the app once you scan a code from the top.  Hasbro’s original price for these tops was apparently about $13.  Prices are higher now, and vary quite a bit among online sellers.

This game is less of a game and more of a "get to know someone" activity. The cards are color coded. You're supposed to start with one color and gradually advance to the next one and so on. There are questions, fill-in-the-blanks, and things to list or answer (not in a question form). There are no right or wrong answers. The further you advance, the more difficult the cards become. The subject matter becomes more challenging and it's more of a hot-seat type card. The set allows you to get to know a person's character rather than learning what someone's favorite hobby is. It's literally the size of a deck of cards. In fact, it is a deck of cards. It's very portable. There's nothing on the cards that is inappropriate. It's just a thoughtful and interesting activity.

This LEGO IDEAS 21319 CENTRAL PERK BUILDING KIT (New 2019; 1,070 Pieces) started out on the LEGO “Ideas” website, where LEGO fans submit their own ideas for builds.  When a build receives 10,000 votes from individual LEGO fans (not as easy as you might think!), LEGO considers it for manufacture and release as a regular LEGO set, made available in retail stores and online.  (To find the site for participation in “Ideas” voting, Google for “Lego Ideas”.)  The Central Perk set is true to the TV show that inspired it. The familiar orange couch is there, along with the coffee shop service bar, and the stage with standing microphone.  Minifigs represent Ross Geller, Rachel Green, Chandler Bing, Monica Geller, Joey Tribbiani, Phoebe Buffay, and Gunther.  The thing I love most about LEGO building sets is the way that the square and rectangular bricks transform as you build into recognizable things (e.g., the orange couch).  I also find that building LEGO sets is relaxing, like assembling a jigsaw puzzle.  As always, the step-by-step LEGO building instructions are crystal clear.  Do note that this Central Perk set has a LOT of small pieces (e.g., coffee cups, single- and double-stud plates, etc.).  This makes it a bit fiddly to build.

Spontuneous is a fresh, clever & fun new family board game. You start by coming up with a list of words. Each word needs to be found in a song. Then once a word (trigger word) is given, the first person to come up with the lyrics that uses that words (in a phrase/line of at least 5 words) gets to roll the dice & advance. My 14 year old daughter LOVED the game. Even after our game was finished, she begged us to keep throwing out trigger words for her. She can't wait to play it with her friends. My 17 year old son is not a fan of most family games and although he did play with us, he couldn't wait to finish. He claimed that he didn't know enough songs to play. My husband & I both enjoyed it, though we did find that the 15 second allotment was hard at times. The directions weren't too complicated which was a big plus! There are challenge cards to get if you land on a certain spot. There was one penalty card which you could use if you caught someone on their cell phone! The game is designed for 4-10 players and is relatively short with games lasting about 30 minutes. It was interesting how we would all come up with different songs for each trigger word!  *I received the game for the purpose of trying out & reviewing. My thoughts & opinions are my own.

Building toys for girls are a good thing, and this MEGA CONSTRUX MONSTER HIGH CARNIVAL BUILDING SET that’s based on the ”Monster High” dolls is an excellent one.  The “Scaris Wheel” isn’t motorized, but it’s a solid build that moves freely (both wheel and hanging gondola chairs).  There are some tricky connections in the wheel, but nothing that’s too hard to figure out.  The included action figures (Cleo de Nile and Deuce Gorgon) are made of semi-soft plastic, jointed, and printed in full color with great detail.  (Yes, the Deuce Gorgon figure has green eyes under those sunglasses”!)  The figures come pre-assembled, but can be taken apart (hair, head, clothing, accessories) and reassembled.  To carry out the carnival theme, Cleo holds a pretzel, and Deuce has an apple on a stick.  Both figures can ride on the Scaris Wheel.  The Scaris Wheel and its supporting tower are decorated with little skulls.  There is a ticket booth that attaches to the wheel tower and swings out (or can be removed to stand alone).  I love the dark-charcoal-and-blue-mottled bricks that are used in the support structures.  In my experience, Mega Bloks are slightly harder to assemble than LEGO bricks.  The don’t seem to be manufactured with the same precision, so they don’t snap together cleanly.   Also, the Mega Bloks assembly directions are harder to follow than LEGO instructions, because they are written as “exploded” add-a-brick diagrams rather than as diagrams showing how the model looks after each brick is added.  Still, learning to understand exploded diagrams will stand kids in good stead when they grow up and start assembling furniture!

One reason to like this SHIMMER & SHINE SPARKLE SPA! 70837 BUILDING KIT (from THE LEGO MOVIE 2; 691 Pieces) is that it includes a lot of the beautiful, sparkly, transparent blue elements previously seen in the LEGO “Frozen” kits.  Another reason to like it is that it includes a rotating platform for the vampire (or another minifig), and a transparent lever for emptying the spa tub (along with Unikitty or any other hapless tub occupant).  Despite the high number of pieces (691), this is not a large model.  Many of the pieces are one- or two-stud elements or specialty elements (e.g., flames) that are mostly added for architectural decoration.  Still, the high piece count means that the model takes a while to build (always a good thing, in my opinion).  As always, the building instructions are easy to follow.  The instructions can also be downloaded to an Apple or Android mobile device.  You use the LEGO Life app to scan the QR code (looks like a maze) that is printed on the box and the instruction booklet.  (The downloaded instructions are exactly the same as the printed ones.)

As a Lost In Space fan as a kid, I had to get this after seeing a video of it. Both my uncle and my father were in the construction industry in NYC/Brooklyn in the 1930's-40's. When I saw this, I remembered the stories I used to hear about how they would dangle from beams on the skyscraper construction. This puzzle is unique that it is in black and white (which is a new, unique challenge for me) and at the same time educational/inspirational for people of all ages. To see how our ancestors had to build the skyscrapers that we all take for granted today, before all the safety precautions of today, is eye-opening. I love puzzles and try to complete one every month or two. I am hooked on black and white now as it brings a whole new dimension to trying to match and fit pieces. I highly recommend this for everyone.

This MATCHBOX DINO TRAPPER TRAILER toy is great fun for me, as an adult.  I love the growling T-Rex sound effects, the glowing red eyes, and the whole operation of shooting a dart and then loading the sleeping dino onto the trailer.  It is quite easy to hit the T-Rex in the chest with a dart, to put him to sleep.  For the loading, you have to collar the T-Rex, attach him to the truck's winch, crank the winch to pull him onto the trailer, and then secure the load with a chain.  A couple of reservations I have:  (1) I'm not sure how long this toy will sustain a child's interest; and (2) I think that attaching the rubbery collar and chain will require more strength and dexterity than a small child's hands are likely to possess.  However, the T-Rex is fun to activate and move around,  and the trapper's truck is a big, solid, detailed toy--a great truck for playing with by itself.  The darts are easily aimed, travel just far enough to be retrieved without effort, and have padded tips that are unlikely to do any damage to anyone who is hit by one.  Getting the T-Rex and trapper's truck out of the box is a bit of a challenge.  There are posts that need to be rotated from the underside of the cardboard, a string to cut, some clamshell packaging (for the darts), and a rubber band around the truck's wheel that secures the winch.  A small child would definitely need help unpacking this toy.  Note that this is a "Matchbox" toy, but not a matchbox-sized toy.  It is a very large toy.  I genuinely love the Dino Trapper Trailer, and want to keep it for myself!  It will be given to a child, though--eventually.

This LEARNING RESOURCES BUILD & SPIN: FARM FRIENDS (17 Pieces) toy is simply adorable.  It’s well-made, with big plastic pieces (no sharp edges).  The gear pieces fit into holes in the base; and when assembled, they turn when the handle (the farmhouse) is turned.  The animal pieces fit atop the gears, and are totally mix-and-match. This BEYBLADE BURST EVOLUTION SWITCHSTRIKE STARTER PACK (Fafnir F3) contains a Beyblade Burst Evolution Fafnir F3 (one of the few left-spin tops), and a launcher that can be used for either left-spin or right-spin tops.  You will need to purchase a “battle stadium” separately (e.g., [[ASIN:B01F6VS3ZG Beyblade Burst Beystadium]]).  Hasbro’s Burst Evolution battling tops are fun toys, but they have a learning curve.  The included instructions are so brief as to be almost useless, though they do explain how to assemble the top.  (There are detailed instructions and videos available on the toy manufacturer’s web site.  Go to the official Hasbro site, choose “Brands”, and then “Beyblade Burst”.)  It took some practice to get a good “Rip” (launch).  It also took me awhile to figure out that the launcher wouldn’t work unless the strip was inserted in the correct slot (e.g., left for a left-spin top), that the top needed to be rotated slightly onto the launcher, and that the tops were meant to be dropped into the stadium (not started on the stadium floor).  The parts for these Burst Evolution tops can be interchanged to create custom tops with different strengths and weaknesses.  I would strongly advise any adult who is purchasing one of these tops for a youngster to find out exactly which model it is that the kid wants.  The tops perform very differently, depending on their weight and on features such as the type of tip (e.g., rollerball).  The back of each toy card gives details on the features (e.g., type (attack or defense), weight, endurance, etc.) of the included top.  These tops require two people and two tops for play, because the tops are intended to be launched simultaneously for each battle.  I think that single players can use the accompanying Beyblade Burst app (available for iOS and Android), which adds the tops you own to your collection in the app, once you scan in a code from the top.

This LEGO STAR WARS FIRST ORDER HEAVY SCOUT WALKER BUILDING KIT (75177; 554 Piece) makes a fun toy—when you push it, wheels hidden underneath make it “crawl” (the legs go up and down); and it shoots long, red, springloaded plastic bolts.  Basically this is a Technic build (using mostly Technic pieces and connectors), which makes it a little more difficult than, say, a City or Creator build (using standard bricks, plates, etc.).  The instructions are crystal clear, as always with LEGO.  The trickiest connections in the build are circled, and there’s no problem as long as you regularly check to see that all the moving parts are still able to move after you add something.  The set comes with four minifigs (General Hux, a First Order Gunner, a Resistance Trooper, and a First Order Flametrooper).  This is an interesting build—it always amazes me, the way that the LEGO master builders use LEGO pieces to replicate machinery and mechanical movements.  With 554 pieces in this set, the $49.99 price seems reasonable to me, especially since LEGO must pay Disney for the use of the Star Wars trademark and toy design.

My 21 year old son is still a Pokemon fan. I thought he might enjoy this. He played a round or two, but then grew bored with it. It's a lot like the 20-Q game where you answer a series of yes and no questions then it guess what you're thinking. Most of the time when she played it, it guessed the correct answer. Since my daughter doesn't know a lot about the Pokemon subject, she found that she wasn't able to keep going after a while. We then shared this with my 8 year old nephew. He also has limited Pokemon knowledge. However, he started researching the Pokemon just so he could stump the gadget. He has been having a blast with it! So, I think if you get the right target audience, then it's a good form of entertainment. There is a finite number of Pokemon that you can work with though, thus limiting the game.

My husband had to re-drill holes to make it straight.

This is a great toy for toddlers and kids, there are lights and cars involved. There is supposed to be music involved but after installing new double A batteries(that I had to provide myself), only the lights work. The one I purchased makes no sound. That’s disappointing

Its a real shame because the cube turns so smoothly, it is lite, and it just feels great. But sadly a center piece popped out. Normally with bigger cubes that I have, if it pops I can get it back in. However I tried and tried until the piece that popped out snapped. The cube still feels pretty much the same but it looks off with a piece missing, and occasionally when I turn the layer with the missing piece, it misaligns and locks up.
It was smashed.

First, none of the subjects that you're supposed to rate them on matter. Easy to learn, easy to read and for traveling don't apply to domino racks. So, I didn't rate them on any of those factors.  I'm rating them on finish and function. I don't know about all the other games you can use them for, such as Mahjong or Mexican Train. But, for regular domino games the racks are too large. Even if you had to draw from the bone pile a lot, in a two-handed game, I don't think you'd need racks this wide.  Then, there's nothing special about them. They're simple pieces of pine molding. No stain, varnish or finish of any kind. Honestly, I think I could have gone to the local builder's supply store and bought a suitable piece of carpentry molding for about $5.  I'm going to keep them because I have them and it's convenient. But, I will always agonize about how much I paid for them, every time I use them.

This camera was way to tiny for a 3-4 year old as it states in the description. I found a better one that was more child friendly and had more options for a better price. This camera  literally fit in the palm of my hand. I also disliked the two offers for a free Amazon gift card ifv I gave a 5 star review and did not mention the offer from the seller in my review. Returned for refund.
